explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled for the procedure.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF is used while transactions occur later, allowing some errors to leave work partially applied.
SELECT * from Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column.
SELECT TOP (10) from Sales.Orders without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary set of rows.
DISTINCT on o.OrderID is unnecessary because OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) wraps the indexed date column and prevents a simple range seek.
o.CustomerID is int but @CustomerIdText is nvarchar, causing implicit conversion in the predicate.
@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID makes cardinality estimation and index seeking difficult.
LIKE N'%urgent%' starts with a wildcard, preventing a normal index seek on Comments.
NOT IN (SELECT i.CustomerID ...) can return no rows if the subquery produces NULL.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) wraps the column in a function and makes the predicate non-sargable.
The predicate compares CustomerID to hard-coded value 1 instead of using a parameter or lookup.
o.OrderID + 0 applies arithmetic to the column and prevents direct index matching.
o.CustomerID <> 1 uses inequality and can scan broad portions of Sales.Orders.
CustomerID value 1 is hard-coded in the predicate.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers multiplies rows with no join predicate.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Comma-separated tables in FROM with the join condition in WHERE use old-style join syntax.
The scalar subquery counting Sales.OrderLines executes once per outer Sales.Orders row.
Correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines runs per outer Sales.Orders row instead of a set join.
The same CASE expression is repeated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 refers to a select-list position and becomes fragile when the select list changes.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by o.OrderDate lacks a stable tie-breaker such as OrderID.
UNION removes duplicates unnecessarily between branches already disjoint by OrderID values.
OrderID value 1 is hard-coded in the first UNION branch.
OrderID value 2 is hard-coded in the second UNION branch.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching rows when only existence is needed.
The correlated scalar COUNT(*) subquery executes per outer Sales.Orders row.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID can silently accept multiple rows and keep the last value.
Table variable @OrderIds can receive poor cardinality estimates, especially on older compatibility levels.
INSERT SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY selects arbitrary rows.
SELECT INTO #ImplicitOrders creates a temp-table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY selects arbitrary rows.
Cursor order_cursor processes Sales.Orders rows one at a time instead of set-based work.
Cursor query SELECT TOP (2) without ORDER BY selects arbitrary rows.
WHILE loop performs iterative processing instead of a set-based operation.
Dynamic SQL is built by concatenating @CustomerID into the command text.
Procedure input @CustomerID is concatenated directly into dynamic SQL before execution.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without parameterized inputs.
Dynamic SQL is built by concatenating @OrderID into the command text.
sys.sp_executesql is called without parameters, leaving values embedded in the SQL text.
#Scratch is explicitly created but not dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE without rethrowing or returning a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path instead of THROW.
PRINT is used for diagnostics, which is buffered and unsuitable for structured logging.
CustomerID value 1 is hard-coded in the predicate.
Date constant '20160101' is embedded directly in query logic.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') omits an explicit style and depends on session date settings.
Date string '01/02/2016' is embedded directly in query logic.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
COALESCE in the join predicate inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Sales.Orders is self-joined only to retrieve the same OrderID already available in the row.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts on a wide nvarchar(max) column.
SUM over Sales.OrderLines aggregates the entire fact-like table without a restrictive filter.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate that belongs in WHERE.
ORDER BY on an aggregate query without GROUP BY sorts a guaranteed single-row result.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
The MERGE UPDATE action sets target.Comments to its current value, causing no data change.
UPDATE sets Comments = Comments, causing logging and locking without changing data.
DELETE based on Quantity = -1 uses a broad non-key predicate.
Quantity value -1 is hard-coded in the DELETE predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY intentionally blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not wrapped in a BEGIN TRY / BEGIN CATCH block.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
sp_who is executed without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes session state and is not restored.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES omits an explicit column list.
CategoryID value 99 is hard-coded in the INSERT values.
INSERT INTO #DemoCustomers SELECT omits an explicit column list.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' starts with a wildcard, preventing index seeks.
Dynamic SQL is built by concatenating @CustomerName into the command text.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into dynamic SQL before execution.
sp_executesql executes dynamic SQL without parameterized inputs.
sp_executesql is executed without a schema qualifier.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers lacks a WHERE clause and affects all rows.
CustomerCategoryID value 1 is hard-coded in the UPDATE SET clause.
Date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded directly in query logic.
WHERE o.OrderDate filters the outer-joined Sales.Orders table, converting the LEFT JOIN to an inner join.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL should use IS NULL; equality to NULL never matches.
dbo.GetCustomerType is called in the SELECT list, forcing row-by-row evaluation.
The CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE without rethrowing or returning a failure status.
The CATCH block does not roll back the open transaction before handling the error.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes an index hint in production code.
The query nests subqueries three or more levels deep and should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
LIKE '%A%' starts with a wildcard, preventing index seeks on CustomerName.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti returns all columns instead of an explicit column list.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is used as a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would optimize better.
